title: A Blood Type Rarer Than 'Golden Blood'? Meet B(A), Found in Just Three Humans
summary: You've probably heard of blood types A, B, AB, and O. But what if your blood type was so rare that only two other people on Earth shared it? In 2025, researchers at a Thai hospital stumbled upon exactly that: a new blood type called B(A), found in just one patient and two donors. It's a discovery that adds a 49th type to the 48 already known in humans—and it's rarer than the legendary 'golden blood' (Rh-null), which only about 50 people have. Blood types are determined by antigens—sugars and proteins on red blood cells that alert your immune system to foreign invaders. The ABO system, discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1901, is based on two main antigens: A (with N-acetylgalactosamine sugar) and B (with D-galactose). Type AB has both, type O has neither, and the Rh factor adds another layer. But B(A) breaks the rules: it's predominantly B, yet carries trace amounts of A antigen. 'ABO discrepancies were distinct between donors and patients even in the same ethnicity,' the researchers wrote in Transfusion and Apheresis Science. They found that all three B(A) individuals carried identical genetic alterations—four alleles (gene variants) on chromosome 9 that differed from any previously known B(A) phenotype. This means the anomaly isn't random; it's a specific, heritable mutation. Why does this matter? In transfusions, mismatched antigens can trigger a dangerous immune attack. Type O negative is the universal donor because it lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, but for someone with B(A), even standard B blood might cause a reaction if the trace A antigen is recognized as foreign. The Thai team's discovery highlights how blood typing isn't as simple as ABO—especially in diverse populations. 'This finding highlighted the influence of the patient's conditions and therapy on the anomalous ABO typing,' they noted, suggesting that illness or treatment might unmask hidden antigens. The hunt is now on for a fourth B(A) carrier. With further research, scientists may uncover even more ultra-rare types, proving that our blood is far more complex—and fascinating—than a simple letter.
why_it_matters: Understanding ultra-rare blood types like B(A) is not just a scientific curiosity — it can be life-saving. As you grow up and possibly become a blood donor or need medical care yourself, knowing that blood typing is more complex than a simple A-B-O label highlights why diversity in donor registries and precise testing matter for everyone’s safety.

keywords.explanation: Molecules (often sugars or proteins) on the surface of cells that can trigger an immune response if they are identified as foreign.
A protein that can be present on red blood cells; if you have it, your blood type is positive, and if you lack it, you are negative.
Different versions of the same gene; they can lead to small variations in a trait, such as a slightly different blood type.
One of the 23 pairs of chromosome packages that hold human DNA; the B(A) mutations were found on this specific chromosome.
The physical or observable trait that results from a gene—in this case, having mostly B-type blood with a tiny bit of A.
A genetic change that can be passed from parent to child, which explains why B(A) ran in the few carriers found.

questions.question: How many people were known to have the B(A) blood type at the time of the discovery?
What makes B(A) unusual compared to normal ABO blood types?
options: It has no antigens at all
It is predominantly B but carries trace A antigen
questions.correct_answer: It is predominantly B but carries trace A antigen

questions.question: Who first discovered the ABO blood group system?
Why is type O negative considered the universal donor?
options: It has extra A and B antigens
It lacks A, B, and Rh antigens
It is the most common blood type
It contains both A and B antigens
questions.correct_answer: It lacks A, B, and Rh antigens

questions.question: What did all three B(A) individuals share genetically?
What can happen in a transfusion if antigens are mismatched?
options: A dangerous immune attack can be triggered
questions.correct_answer: A dangerous immune attack can be triggered

background_read: The ABO blood group system was first described in 1901 by Karl Landsteiner (the Austrian biologist who later won a Nobel Prize for this discovery). Landsteiner noticed that mixing blood from different people sometimes caused clotting, leading him to identify the A, B, and O types. The Rh factor was uncovered later, in 1940, by the same scientist and others while studying rhesus monkeys — its presence or absence adds the '+' or '-' label.

So-called 'golden blood' (Rh-null) is exceptionally rare because it lacks all Rh antigens, making it a universal donor for anyone with a rare Rh system type. Only about 50 people worldwide are known to have it. B(A) is even rarer, with just three confirmed carriers at the time the discovery was reported in 2025 in the journal Transfusion and Apheresis Science.

Blood typing is more complex than just ABO and Rh; there are over 30 recognized blood group systems (like Kell, Duffy, and Kidd). Some rare types are much more common in certain ethnic groups, which is why diverse blood donor registries are important for safe transfusions.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Discovery of the B(A) blood type, only the 49th ever documented and rarer than golden blood.
└─ specific: 'just one patient and two donors' shared it.
BACKGROUND: Blood types are determined by antigens; the ABO system classifies A, B, AB, and O based on two main sugars, and Rh factor adds another layer.
ANOMALY: B(A) breaks the rules — it is mostly B but carries tiny amounts of A antigen.
└─ quote: 'ABO discrepancies were distinct between donors and patients even in the same ethnicity.'
GENETIC EVIDENCE: All three carriers had identical, never-before-seen alleles on chromosome 9, proving a specific heritable mutation.
REAL-WORLD RISK: Mismatched antigens can cause a dangerous immune attack; even standard B blood might be unsafe for a B(A) person.
└─ note: illness or therapy might unmask hidden antigens, complicating typing.
FORWARD VIEW: Scientists are now searching for a fourth carrier and expect to find more ultra-rare types.

perspectives.description: The discovery improves transfusion medicine by alerting doctors to a previously unknown type that could cause reactions. It also adds to genetic knowledge about how blood types diversify.

The fact that such a rare type went undetected for so long raises concern that other dangerous mismatches might still be unrecognized, and that current blood screening may miss similar anomalies, especially in underserved populations.

The B(A) case illustrates the limitations of simple ABO classification. It shows that blood group phenotypes are a spectrum influenced by multiple alleles, and that evolution continues to produce novel variations in human populations.

As more ultra-rare types are found, there may be a need for national or global registries of donors with extreme blood types. This could lead to more personalized matching, just as we see with tissue typing for organ transplants.
